ax1.axvline(x=120, color='orange', linestyle='--', label='End of winter (120 days)')
ax1.legend()
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Bottom: Cumulative volume
ax2.plot(t, V, linewidth=2, color='green', label='Cumulative volume V(t)')
ax2.axhline(y=1500, color='r', linestyle='--', label='Target (1500 ML)')
ax2.axvline(x=120, color='orange', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
ax2.set_xlabel('Days since start')
ax2.set_ylabel('Volume (ML)')
ax2.set_title('Total Water Infiltrated')
ax2.legend()
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
7

Week 7: Definite Integrals & Economic Surplus

Market welfare · Consumer & producer surplus · Net change

Week 7: Definite Integrals and Economic Surplus

📝 ALL GROUPS: Lab Activity

During this week's lab, ALL groups work on the following problem:

PROBLEM BRIEF: W7

"Market Welfare: Who Benefits from Trade?"

SCENARIO

The Western Australian grain market is being analyzed for a policy review. Economists have estimated the following supply and demand functions for wheat:

Demand function: Q d = 500 − 4 P Q_d = 500 - 4P Qd​=500−4P (quantity in thousands of tonnes, price in $/tonne)

Supply function: Q s = − 100 + 2 P Q_s = -100 + 2P Qs​=−100+2P

The government is considering a price support policy and needs to understand how the market currently distributes benefits between consumers and producers.

Key concepts:

  • Consumer Surplus (CS): The benefit consumers receive from paying less than their maximum willingness to pay
  • Producer Surplus (PS): The benefit producers receive from selling at a price higher than their minimum acceptable price
  • Total Welfare: CS + PS

YOUR TASK

Part A: Market Equilibrium (15 minutes)

  1. Find the equilibrium price P ∗ P^* P∗ by setting Q d = Q s Q_d = Q_s Qd​=Qs​.

  2. Find the equilibrium quantity Q ∗ Q^* Q∗.

  3. Find the maximum willingness to pay (price at which Q d = 0 Q_d = 0 Qd​=0).

  4. Find the minimum acceptable price (price at which Q s = 0 Q_s = 0 Qs​=0).

Part B: Setting Up the Surplus Integrals (20 minutes)

To calculate surplus, we need the inverse demand and supply functions (price as a function of quantity):

  1. Rearrange Q d = 500 − 4 P Q_d = 500 - 4P Qd​=500−4P to get P = D ( Q ) P = D(Q) P=D(Q) (inverse demand).

  2. Rearrange Q s = − 100 + 2 P Q_s = -100 + 2P Qs​=−100+2P to get P = S ( Q ) P = S(Q) P=S(Q) (inverse supply).

  3. Consumer Surplus formula:

    C S = ∫ 0 Q ∗ D ( Q )   d Q − P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ CS = \int_0^{Q^*} D(Q)\,dQ - P^* \cdot Q^* CS=∫0Q∗​D(Q)dQ−P∗⋅Q∗

    Explain in words what each term represents. Why do we subtract P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ P^* \cdot Q^* P∗⋅Q∗?

  4. Producer Surplus formula:

    P S = P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ − ∫ 0 Q ∗ S ( Q )   d Q PS = P^* \cdot Q^* - \int_0^{Q^*} S(Q)\,dQ PS=P∗⋅Q∗−∫0Q∗​S(Q)dQ

    Explain in words what each term represents.

Part C: Calculating Surplus (20 minutes)

  1. Calculate Consumer Surplus using the integral formula. Show all steps.

  2. Calculate Producer Surplus using the integral formula. Show all steps.

  3. Calculate Total Welfare = CS + PS.

  4. Shortcut verification: For linear demand and supply, the surplus areas are triangles:

    • C S = 1 2 × Q ∗ × ( P m a x − P ∗ ) CS = \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P_{max} - P^*) CS=21​×Q∗×(Pmax​−P∗)
    • P S = 1 2 × Q ∗ × ( P ∗ − P m i n ) PS = \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P^* - P_{min}) PS=21​×Q∗×(P∗−Pmin​)

    Verify your integral calculations match these triangle formulas.

Part D: Policy Analysis (if time permits)

  1. The government proposes a price floor of $120/tonne (above equilibrium).
    • At P = 120 P = 120 P=120, what quantity do consumers demand?
    • At P = 120 P = 120 P=120, what quantity do producers supply?
    • What problem does this create?

DELIVERABLES

  • Completed Group Worksheet
  • Groups 4 & 5: Prepare 10-minute presentation for Week 8

PRESENTATION GUIDANCE (Groups 4 & 5)

Focus: How integration quantifies economic welfare

Suggested visuals:

  • Supply and demand curves with equilibrium marked
  • Shaded areas showing CS and PS
  • Comparison: integral method vs. geometry

HINTS & GUIDED STEPS

Integration reminder:

∫ ( a x + b )   d x = a 2 x 2 + b x + C \int (ax + b)\,dx = \frac{a}{2}x^2 + bx + C ∫(ax+b)dx=2a​x2+bx+C

Definite integral:

∫ a b f ( x )   d x = F ( b ) − F ( a ) \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a) ∫ab​f(x)dx=F(b)−F(a)

where F ( x ) F(x) F(x) is an antiderivative of f ( x ) f(x) f(x).

Python template:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Q = np.linspace(0, 500, 500)

# Inverse functions
P_demand = (500 - Q) / 4
P_supply = (Q + 100) / 2

# Equilibrium
Q_star = 200  # From Qd = Qs
P_star = 100

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.plot(Q, P_demand, linewidth=2, label='Demand', color='blue')
plt.plot(Q, P_supply, linewidth=2, label='Supply', color='red')
plt.plot(Q_star, P_star, 'go', markersize=12, label=f'Equilibrium (Q={Q_star}, P={P_star})')

# Consumer surplus area
Q_cs = np.linspace(0, Q_star, 100)
P_cs = (500 - Q_cs) / 4
plt.fill_between(Q_cs, P_star, P_cs, alpha=0.3, color='blue', label='Consumer Surplus')

# Producer surplus area
Q_ps = np.linspace(0, Q_star, 100)
P_ps = (Q_ps + 100) / 2
plt.fill_between(Q_ps, P_ps, P_star, alpha=0.3, color='red', label='Producer Surplus')

plt.xlabel('Quantity (thousands of tonnes)')
plt.ylabel('Price ($/tonne)')
plt.title('Wheat Market: Consumer and Producer Surplus')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

🎤 PRESENTATION PROBLEMS: For Groups Presenting Next Week

The following problems are for the two groups presenting next week. Work on your assigned variation during this week's lab to prepare your presentation.

PROBLEM BRIEF: W7A (for Group 4 second presentation in Week 8)

"Wine Market Analysis: Assessing Regional Wine Policy"

SCENARIO

The Margaret River Wine Association is evaluating the impact of a proposed minimum price policy. Economic consultants have estimated supply and demand for premium wine (measured in thousands of bottles per year):

Demand: Q d = 800 − 5 P Q_d = 800 - 5P Qd​=800−5P (quantity demanded, price in $/bottle)
Supply: Q s = − 200 + 3 P Q_s = -200 + 3P Qs​=−200+3P (quantity supplied)

The Association wants to understand who benefits under current free-market conditions before implementing price controls.

YOUR TASK

Part A: Market Equilibrium (15 minutes)

  1. Find equilibrium price P ∗ P^* P∗ by setting Q d = Q s Q_d = Q_s Qd​=Qs​

  2. Find equilibrium quantity Q ∗ Q^* Q∗

  3. Calculate:

    • Maximum willingness to pay (price where Q d = 0 Q_d = 0 Qd​=0)
    • Minimum acceptable price for producers (price where Q s = 0 Q_s = 0 Qs​=0)

Part B: Inverse Functions for Integration (15 minutes)

  1. Rearrange demand to get inverse demand: P = D ( Q ) P = D(Q) P=D(Q)

    • From Q = 800 − 5 P Q = 800 - 5P Q=800−5P, solve for P P P

  2. Rearrange supply to get inverse supply: P = S ( Q ) P = S(Q) P=S(Q)

    • From Q = − 200 + 3 P Q = -200 + 3P Q=−200+3P, solve for P P P

  3. Verify your inverse functions:

    • Substitute Q ∗ Q^* Q∗ into D ( Q ) D(Q) D(Q) — do you get P ∗ P^* P∗?
    • Substitute Q ∗ Q^* Q∗ into S ( Q ) S(Q) S(Q) — do you get P ∗ P^* P∗?

Part C: Calculating Surplus (25 minutes)

  1. Consumer Surplus formula:

    C S = ∫ 0 Q ∗ D ( Q )   d Q − P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ CS = \int_0^{Q^*} D(Q)\,dQ - P^* \cdot Q^* CS=∫0Q∗​D(Q)dQ−P∗⋅Q∗

    Explain: What does each term represent?

    • The integral ∫ 0 Q ∗ D ( Q )   d Q \int_0^{Q^*} D(Q)\,dQ ∫0Q∗​D(Q)dQ = ?
    • The rectangle P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ P^* \cdot Q^* P∗⋅Q∗ = ?
    • The difference = ?

  2. Calculate Consumer Surplus:

    • Evaluate the definite integral
    • Subtract P ∗ Q ∗ P^* Q^* P∗Q∗
    • Express in dollars

  3. Producer Surplus formula:

    P S = P ∗ ⋅ Q ∗ − ∫ 0 Q ∗ S ( Q )   d Q PS = P^* \cdot Q^* - \int_0^{Q^*} S(Q)\,dQ PS=P∗⋅Q∗−∫0Q∗​S(Q)dQ

    Calculate Producer Surplus using integration.

  4. Verification using geometry:

    • CS = 1 2 × Q ∗ × ( P m a x − P ∗ ) \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P_{max} - P^*) 21​×Q∗×(Pmax​−P∗)
    • PS = 1 2 × Q ∗ × ( P ∗ − P m i n ) \frac{1}{2} \times Q^* \times (P^* - P_{min}) 21​×Q∗×(P∗−Pmin​)
    • Do your integration answers match?

  5. Calculate Total Welfare = CS + PS

    • Who benefits more, consumers or producers?
    • Express CS and PS as percentages of total welfare

Part D: Policy Impact (5 minutes)

  1. The Association proposes a minimum price of P f l o o r = 140 P_{floor} = 140 Pfloor​=140 (above equilibrium).
    • At this price, how much do consumers demand? Q d ( 140 ) = ? Q_d(140) = ? Qd​(140)=?
    • At this price, how much do producers supply? Q s ( 140 ) = ? Q_s(140) = ? Qs​(140)=?
    • What problem arises?

DELIVERABLES

  • Worksheet with all integration steps
  • Graph showing supply, demand, and shaded surplus areas
  • Group 4 (second presentation) in Week 8

PYTHON TEMPLATE

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Quantity range
Q = np.linspace(0, 800, 500)

# Inverse demand and supply
P_demand = (800 - Q) / 5
P_supply = (Q + 200) / 3

# Equilibrium
Q_star = 500  # Solve Qd = Qs
P_star = 60   # Substitute back

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.plot(Q, P_demand, linewidth=2, label='Demand P=D(Q)', color='blue')
plt.plot(Q, P_supply, linewidth=2, label='Supply P=S(Q)', color='red')

# Equilibrium point
plt.plot(Q_star, P_star, 'go', markersize=12, label=f'Equilibrium (Q*={Q_star}, P*=${P_star})')

# Consumer surplus (area above P*, below demand)
Q_cs = np.linspace(0, Q_star, 200)
P_cs = (800 - Q_cs) / 5
plt.fill_between(Q_cs, P_star, P_cs, alpha=0.3, color='blue', label='Consumer Surplus')

# Producer surplus (area below P*, above supply)
Q_ps = np.linspace(0, Q_star, 200)
P_ps = (Q_ps + 200) / 3
plt.fill_between(Q_ps, P_ps, P_star, alpha=0.3, color='red', label='Producer Surplus')

plt.xlabel('Quantity (thousands of bottles)')
plt.ylabel('Price ($/bottle)')
plt.title('Margaret River Wine Market: Consumer and Producer Surplus')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 800)
plt.ylim(0, 200)
plt.show()

PROBLEM BRIEF: W7B (for Group 5 second presentation in Week 8)

"Avocado Market: Export vs. Domestic Trade-offs"

SCENARIO

Australian avocado growers are debating whether to support expanded export access to Asia. Economists have modeled the domestic market:

Demand (domestic): Q d = 600 − 4 P Q_d = 600 - 4P Qd​=600−4P (quantity in tonnes per week, price in $/kg)
Supply (domestic): Q s = − 120 + 2 P Q_s = -120 + 2P Qs​=−120+2P

Opening export markets would effectively increase demand, shifting the curve outward. Growers benefit from higher prices, but consumers face higher costs.

YOUR TASK

Part A: Current Domestic Equilibrium (15 minutes)

  1. Solve for equilibrium price P ∗ P^* P∗ and quantity Q ∗ Q^* Q∗

  2. Find:

    • Maximum consumer willingness to pay
    • Minimum producer acceptable price

  3. Sketch a rough supply-demand diagram

Part B: Surplus Under Current Policy (25 minutes)

  1. Find inverse demand and supply functions:

    • P = D ( Q ) P = D(Q) P=D(Q) from Q d = 600 − 4 P Q_d = 600 - 4P Qd​=600−4P
    • P = S ( Q ) P = S(Q) P=S(Q) from Q s = − 120 + 2 P Q_s = -120 + 2P Qs​=−120+2P

  2. Calculate Consumer Surplus:

    C S = ∫ 0 Q ∗ D ( Q )   d Q − P ∗ Q ∗ CS = \int_0^{Q^*} D(Q)\,dQ - P^* Q^* CS=∫0Q∗​D(Q)dQ−P∗Q∗

    • Evaluate the integral ∫ D ( Q )   d Q \int D(Q)\,dQ ∫D(Q)dQ
    • Apply limits [0, Q ∗ Q^* Q∗]
    • Subtract P ∗ Q ∗ P^* Q^* P∗Q∗

  3. Calculate Producer Surplus:

    P S = P ∗ Q ∗ − ∫ 0 Q ∗ S ( Q )   d Q PS = P^* Q^* - \int_0^{Q^*} S(Q)\,dQ PS=P∗Q∗−∫0Q∗​S(Q)dQ

  4. Verify using triangle formulas:

    • CS = 1 2 Q ∗ ( P m a x − P ∗ ) \frac{1}{2} Q^* (P_{max} - P^*) 21​Q∗(Pmax​−P∗)
    • PS = 1 2 Q ∗ ( P ∗ − P m i n ) \frac{1}{2} Q^* (P^* - P_{min}) 21​Q∗(P∗−Pmin​)

  5. Calculate Total Welfare and the ratio CS/PS

Part C: Export Scenario (15 minutes)

  1. With export access, demand shifts to Q d n e w = 720 − 4 P Q_d^{new} = 720 - 4P Qdnew​=720−4P:

    • Find new equilibrium price P n e w P^{new} Pnew and quantity Q n e w Q^{new} Qnew
    • How much has price increased?

  2. Calculate new Consumer Surplus and Producer Surplus:

    • Use the same integration approach
    • Compare to the baseline scenario

  3. Who wins and loses from export expansion?

    • Change in CS = ?
    • Change in PS = ?
    • Change in Total Welfare = ?

Part D: Equity Consideration (5 minutes)

  1. Export policy creates:

    • Losers: domestic consumers (pay higher prices)
    • Winners: producers (receive higher prices)

    Write a brief (3-4 sentence) policy recommendation considering:

    • Economic efficiency (total welfare)
    • Equity (distribution between groups)
    • Whether compensation mechanisms could address distributional concerns

DELIVERABLES

  • Worksheet showing integration for both scenarios
  • Comparative graph: domestic-only vs. export market
  • Group 5 (second presentation) in Week 8

PYTHON TEMPLATE

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Q = np.linspace(0, 600, 500)

# Domestic-only scenario
P_demand_dom = (600 - Q) / 4
P_supply = (Q + 120) / 2

# Export scenario
P_demand_export = (720 - Q) / 4

# Equilibria
Q_dom = 240  # Solve Qd = Qs
P_dom = 90

Q_export = 260  # Solve Qd_new = Qs
P_export = 115

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

# Left: Domestic-only
ax1.plot(Q, P_demand_dom, linewidth=2, label='Demand (domestic)', color='blue')
ax1.plot(Q, P_supply, linewidth=2, label='Supply', color='red')
ax1.plot(Q_dom, P_dom, 'go', markersize=10, label=f'Equilibrium (Q={Q_dom}, P=${P_dom})')

Q_range = np.linspace(0, Q_dom, 200)
P_d = (600 - Q_range) / 4
P_s = (Q_range + 120) / 2
ax1.fill_between(Q_range, P_dom, P_d, alpha=0.3, color='blue', label='CS')
ax1.fill_between(Q_range, P_s, P_dom, alpha=0.3, color='red', label='PS')

ax1.set_xlabel('Quantity (tonnes/week)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Price ($/kg)')
ax1.set_title('Domestic Market Only')
ax1.legend()
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Right: Export scenario
ax2.plot(Q, P_demand_export, linewidth=2, label='Demand (with exports)', color='darkblue')
ax2.plot(Q, P_supply, linewidth=2, label='Supply', color='red')
ax2.plot(Q_export, P_export, 'mo', markersize=10, label=f'New Eq (Q={Q_export}, P=${P_export})')

Q_range2 = np.linspace(0, Q_export, 200)
P_d2 = (720 - Q_range2) / 4
P_s2 = (Q_range2 + 120) / 2
ax2.fill_between(Q_range2, P_export, P_d2, alpha=0.3, color='blue', label='CS (reduced)')
ax2.fill_between(Q_range2, P_s2, P_export, alpha=0.3, color='red', label='PS (increased)')

ax2.set_xlabel('Quantity (tonnes/week)')
ax2.set_ylabel('Price ($/kg)')
ax2.set_title('With Export Access')
ax2.legend()
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
8

Week 8: Lotka-Volterra & Predator-Prey

Predator-prey dynamics · Phase portraits · Equilibria

Week 8: Lotka-Volterra and Predator-Prey Dynamics

📝 ALL GROUPS: Lab Activity

During this week's lab, ALL groups work on the following problem:

PROBLEM BRIEF: W8

"Feral Cat Management: Protecting Native Wildlife"

SCENARIO

The Western Shield program aims to protect endangered numbats from feral cats. Ecologists have developed a predator-prey model:

d N d t = 0.4 N − 0.002 N C d C d t = − 0.3 C + 0.001 N C \begin{aligned} \frac{dN}{dt} &= 0.4N - 0.002NC \\ \frac{dC}{dt} &= -0.3C + 0.001NC \end{aligned} dtdN​dtdC​​=0.4N−0.002NC=−0.3C+0.001NC​

where:

  • N N N = numbat population
  • C C C = feral cat population
  • t t t = time (years)

Model interpretation:

  • Numbats grow at 40% per year without cats
  • Each cat-numbat interaction reduces numbat growth
  • Cats decline at 30% per year without prey
  • Predation increases cat population

YOUR TASK

Part A: Equilibrium Analysis (25 minutes)

  1. Find the trivial equilibrium (both species extinct):

    • Set N = 0 , C = 0 N = 0, C = 0 N=0,C=0

  2. Find the coexistence equilibrium:

    • Set d N d t = 0 \frac{dN}{dt} = 0 dtdN​=0: N ( 0.4 − 0.002 C ) = 0 N(0.4 - 0.002C) = 0 N(0.4−0.002C)=0
      • Either N = 0 N = 0 N=0 or C = ? C = ? C=?
    • Set d C d t = 0 \frac{dC}{dt} = 0 dtdC​=0: C ( − 0.3 + 0.001 N ) = 0 C(-0.3 + 0.001N) = 0 C(−0.3+0.001N)=0
      • Either C = 0 C = 0 C=0 or N = ? N = ? N=?
    • Coexistence: ( N ∗ , C ∗ ) = ( ? , ? ) (N^*, C^*) = (?, ?) (N∗,C∗)=(?,?)

  3. Interpret: What does this equilibrium mean for wildlife management?

Part B: Phase Plane Analysis (20 minutes)

  1. Nullclines (curves where population growth = 0):

    • Numbat nullcline: d N d t = 0 \frac{dN}{dt} = 0 dtdN​=0 → N = 0 N = 0 N=0 or C = ? C = ? C=?
    • Cat nullcline: d C d t = 0 \frac{dC}{dt} = 0 dtdC​=0 → C = 0 C = 0 C=0 or N = ? N = ? N=?

  2. The phase plane is divided into four regions:

    Region Condition d N d t \frac{dN}{dt} dtdN​ d C d t \frac{dC}{dt} dtdC​ Movement
    I N < N ∗ , C < C ∗ N < N^*, C < C^* N<N∗,C<C∗ ? ? ?
    II N > N ∗ , C < C ∗ N > N^*, C < C^* N>N∗,C<C∗ ? ? ?
    III N > N ∗ , C > C ∗ N > N^*, C > C^* N>N∗,C>C∗ ? ? ?
    IV N < N ∗ , C > C ∗ N < N^*, C > C^* N<N∗,C>C∗ ? ? ?

    Fill in the table.

  3. Sketch the phase portrait showing how populations move in each region.

Part C: Management Interventions (15 minutes)

  1. Cat culling: Remove 50 cats per year

    • Modified equation: d C d t = − 0.3 C + 0.001 N C − 50 \frac{dC}{dt} = -0.3C + 0.001NC - 50 dtdC​=−0.3C+0.001NC−50
    • Find the new equilibrium
    • How does numbat population change?

  2. Numbat translocation: Add 20 numbats per year

    • Modified equation: d N d t = 0.4 N − 0.002 N C + 20 \frac{dN}{dt} = 0.4N - 0.002NC + 20 dtdN​=0.4N−0.002NC+20
    • Find the new equilibrium
    • Compare effectiveness to cat culling

  3. Which intervention better supports numbat conservation? Why?

Part D: Ecological Reflection (5 minutes, if time permits)

  1. Combined intervention: Suppose the wildlife agency applies both strategies simultaneously: culling 50 feral cats per year AND translocating 20 numbats per year.

    • Write the modified system of equations
    • Find the new equilibrium
    • Compare with Q7 and Q8 — is the combined strategy better than either alone?

DELIVERABLES

  • Completed Group Worksheet with equilibrium analysis
  • Phase plane diagram with nullclines and direction arrows
  • Groups 6 & 7: Prepare 10-minute presentation for Week 9

PRESENTATION GUIDANCE (Groups 6 & 7)

Focus on:

  1. The Lotka-Volterra model structure
  2. Equilibrium concepts (trivial vs. coexistence)
  3. Phase plane analysis and population dynamics
  4. Management implications

HINTS & GUIDED STEPS

To find equilibria, factor equations:

  • N ( 0.4 − 0.002 C ) = 0 N(0.4 - 0.002C) = 0 N(0.4−0.002C)=0 means N = 0 N = 0 N=0 OR 0.4 − 0.002 C = 0 0.4 - 0.002C = 0 0.4−0.002C=0
  • Solve the second equation for C C C

Python template:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Equilibrium
N_star = 300
C_star = 200

# Nullclines
N_range = np.linspace(0, 600, 100)
C_range = np.linspace(0, 400, 100)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.axhline(y=C_star, color='blue', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label=f'Numbat nullcline (C={C_star})')
plt.axvline(x=N_star, color='red', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label=f'Cat nullcline (N={N_star})')
plt.plot(N_star, C_star, 'go', markersize=15, label=f'Equilibrium ({N_star}, {C_star})')

# Direction field
N_grid = np.linspace(50, 550, 15)
C_grid = np.linspace(50, 350, 12)
N_mesh, C_mesh = np.meshgrid(N_grid, C_grid)

dN_dt = 0.4 * N_mesh - 0.002 * N_mesh * C_mesh
dC_dt = -0.3 * C_mesh + 0.001 * N_mesh * C_mesh

# Normalize arrows
magnitude = np.sqrt(dN_dt**2 + dC_dt**2)
dN_norm = dN_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)
dC_norm = dC_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)

plt.quiver(N_mesh, C_mesh, dN_norm, dC_norm, alpha=0.5)

plt.xlabel('Numbat Population (N)')
plt.ylabel('Feral Cat Population (C)')
plt.title('Predator-Prey Phase Plane')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 600)
plt.ylim(0, 400)
plt.show()

🎤 PRESENTATION PROBLEMS: For Groups Presenting Next Week

The following problems are for the two groups presenting next week. Work on your assigned variation during this week's lab to prepare your presentation.

PROBLEM BRIEF: W8A (for Group 6 second presentation in Week 9)

"Fox Control on the Mainland: Protecting Quokka Colonies"

SCENARIO

On the Australian mainland, quokka (Setonix brachyurus) populations face severe predation pressure from the European red fox (Vulpes vulpes). While quokkas thrive on Rottnest Island (which has no foxes), mainland colonies are critically endangered. Ecologists model this predator-prey system:

d Q d t = 0.3 Q − 0.003 Q F d F d t = − 0.2 F + 0.0005 Q F \begin{aligned} \frac{dQ}{dt} &= 0.3Q - 0.003QF \\ \frac{dF}{dt} &= -0.2F + 0.0005QF \end{aligned} dtdQ​dtdF​​=0.3Q−0.003QF=−0.2F+0.0005QF​

where:

  • Q Q Q = quokka population (number of individuals)
  • F F F = fox population (number of individuals)
  • t t t = time in years

Model features:

  • Quokkas grow at 30% per year without predation (moderate reproductive rate)
  • Foxes decline at 20% per year without prey
  • Predation interactions affect both populations

YOUR TASK

Part A: Finding Equilibria (25 minutes)

  1. Trivial equilibrium:

    • ( Q , F ) = ( 0 , 0 ) (Q, F) = (0, 0) (Q,F)=(0,0) — both species absent from the mainland
    • Is this realistic given the current state of mainland populations?

  2. Coexistence equilibrium:

    • Set d Q d t = 0 \frac{dQ}{dt} = 0 dtdQ​=0: Q ( 0.3 − 0.003 F ) = 0 Q(0.3 - 0.003F) = 0 Q(0.3−0.003F)=0
      • Either Q = 0 Q = 0 Q=0 or F = 100 F = 100 F=100
    • Set d F d t = 0 \frac{dF}{dt} = 0 dtdF​=0: F ( − 0.2 + 0.0005 Q ) = 0 F(-0.2 + 0.0005Q) = 0 F(−0.2+0.0005Q)=0
      • Either F = 0 F = 0 F=0 or Q = 400 Q = 400 Q=400
    • Equilibrium: ( Q ∗ , F ∗ ) = ( 400 , 100 ) (Q^*, F^*) = (400, 100) (Q∗,F∗)=(400,100)

  3. Stability consideration:

    • Are populations close to equilibrium stable?
    • Or do they cycle (oscillate)?
    • In this model, small perturbations cause oscillations

Part B: Nullclines and Phase Portrait (20 minutes)

  1. Nullclines:

    • Quokka nullcline: Q = 0 Q = 0 Q=0 or F = 100 F = 100 F=100
    • Fox nullcline: F = 0 F = 0 F=0 or Q = 400 Q = 400 Q=400

  2. Create a phase plane table:

    Region Q Q Q vs. 400 F F F vs. 100 d Q d t \frac{dQ}{dt} dtdQ​ d F d t \frac{dF}{dt} dtdF​ Trajectory
    I Q < 400 Q < 400 Q<400 F < 100 F < 100 F<100 + − Q increases, F decreases
    II Q > 400 Q > 400 Q>400 F < 100 F < 100 F<100 + + Both increase
    III Q > 400 Q > 400 Q>400 F > 100 F > 100 F>100 − + Q decreases, F increases
    IV Q < 400 Q < 400 Q<400 F > 100 F > 100 F>100 − − Both decrease

  3. Sketch the cyclic behavior:

    • Trajectories spiral around the equilibrium
    • High quokkas → foxes increase → quokkas decline → foxes decline → cycle repeats

Part C: Management Scenarios (15 minutes)

  1. Fox baiting program: 1080 poison baiting removes 10 foxes/year

    • New equation: d F d t = − 0.2 F + 0.0005 Q F − 10 \frac{dF}{dt} = -0.2F + 0.0005QF - 10 dtdF​=−0.2F+0.0005QF−10
    • Find new equilibrium: − 0.2 F + 0.0005 Q F − 10 = 0 -0.2F + 0.0005QF - 10 = 0 −0.2F+0.0005QF−10=0
    • From prey nullcline: F = 100 F = 100 F=100 still. Substitute: − 0.2 ( 100 ) + 0.0005 Q ( 100 ) − 10 = 0 -0.2(100) + 0.0005Q(100) - 10 = 0 −0.2(100)+0.0005Q(100)−10=0
    • Solve: − 20 + 0.05 Q − 10 = 0    ⟹    Q = 600 -20 + 0.05Q - 10 = 0 \implies Q = 600 −20+0.05Q−10=0⟹Q=600
    • New equilibrium: ( Q ∗ , F ∗ ) = ( 600 , 100 ) (Q^*, F^*) = (600, 100) (Q∗,F∗)=(600,100)

  2. Quokka translocation: Reintroduce 15 quokkas/year from Rottnest Island

    • New equation: d Q d t = 0.3 Q − 0.003 Q F + 15 \frac{dQ}{dt} = 0.3Q - 0.003QF + 15 dtdQ​=0.3Q−0.003QF+15
    • From predator nullcline: Q = 400 Q = 400 Q=400 still. Substitute: 0.3 ( 400 ) − 0.003 ( 400 ) F + 15 = 0 0.3(400) - 0.003(400)F + 15 = 0 0.3(400)−0.003(400)F+15=0
    • Solve: 120 − 1.2 F + 15 = 0    ⟹    F = 112.5 120 - 1.2F + 15 = 0 \implies F = 112.5 120−1.2F+15=0⟹F=112.5
    • New equilibrium: ( Q ∗ , F ∗ ) = ( 400 , 112.5 ) (Q^*, F^*) = (400, 112.5) (Q∗,F∗)=(400,112.5)

  3. Compare the two strategies:

    • Fox baiting: Quokkas increase 50% (400 → 600), foxes unchanged at 100
    • Quokka translocation: Quokkas stay at 400, foxes increase to 112.5 — the extra quokkas just feed more foxes!
    • Write a brief (3-4 sentence) recommendation explaining why predator control is more effective than prey supplementation for this system

DELIVERABLES

  • Completed worksheet with equilibria and nullclines
  • Phase portrait showing cyclic trajectories
  • Group 6 (second presentation) in Week 9

PYTHON TEMPLATE

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.integrate import odeint

# Define the system
def predator_prey(state, t):
    Q, F = state
    dQ_dt = 0.3 * Q - 0.003 * Q * F
    dF_dt = -0.2 * F + 0.0005 * Q * F
    return [dQ_dt, dF_dt]

# Equilibrium
Q_star, F_star = 400, 100

# Phase plane
Q_range = np.linspace(50, 800, 15)
F_range = np.linspace(10, 250, 15)
Q_mesh, F_mesh = np.meshgrid(Q_range, F_range)

dQ_dt = 0.3 * Q_mesh - 0.003 * Q_mesh * F_mesh
dF_dt = -0.2 * F_mesh + 0.0005 * Q_mesh * F_mesh

# Normalize
magnitude = np.sqrt(dQ_dt**2 + dF_dt**2)
dQ_norm = dQ_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)
dF_norm = dF_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

# Nullclines
plt.axhline(y=100, color='blue', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='Quokka nullcline (F=100)')
plt.axvline(x=400, color='red', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='Fox nullcline (Q=400)')
plt.plot(Q_star, F_star, 'go', markersize=15, label=f'Equilibrium ({Q_star}, {F_star})')

# Vector field
plt.quiver(Q_mesh, F_mesh, dQ_norm, dF_norm, alpha=0.5)

# Add a trajectory
t = np.linspace(0, 50, 1000)
initial_state = [500, 60]
trajectory = odeint(predator_prey, initial_state, t)
plt.plot(trajectory[:, 0], trajectory[:, 1], 'purple', linewidth=1.5, label='Sample trajectory')

plt.xlabel('Quokka Population (Q)')
plt.ylabel('Fox Population (F)')
plt.title('Predator-Prey Phase Plane: Quokkas vs. Foxes')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 900)
plt.ylim(0, 300)
plt.show()

PROBLEM BRIEF: W8B (for Group 7 second presentation in Week 9)

"Dingo Conservation: Managing Kangaroo Populations"

SCENARIO

In arid Australia, dingoes (Canis dingo) are apex predators that regulate kangaroo populations. Removal of dingoes often leads to kangaroo overabundance and rangeland degradation. Ecologists model this system:

d K d t = 0.5 K − 0.0015 K D d D d t = − 0.25 D + 0.0008 K D \begin{aligned} \frac{dK}{dt} &= 0.5K - 0.0015KD \\ \frac{dD}{dt} &= -0.25D + 0.0008KD \end{aligned} dtdK​dtdD​​=0.5K−0.0015KD=−0.25D+0.0008KD​

where:

  • K K K = kangaroo population (number of individuals)
  • D D D = dingo population (number of individuals)
  • t t t = time in years

Model features:

  • Kangaroos grow at 50% per year without predation (high reproductive rate)
  • Dingoes decline at 25% per year without prey
  • Predation interactions affect both populations

YOUR TASK

Part A: Finding Equilibria (25 minutes)

  1. Trivial equilibrium:

    • ( K , D ) = ( 0 , 0 ) (K, D) = (0, 0) (K,D)=(0,0) — both species extinct
    • Is this realistic for arid Australia?

  2. Coexistence equilibrium:

    • Set d K d t = 0 \frac{dK}{dt} = 0 dtdK​=0: K ( 0.5 − 0.0015 D ) = 0 K(0.5 - 0.0015D) = 0 K(0.5−0.0015D)=0
      • Either K = 0 K = 0 K=0 or D = 333.33 D = 333.33 D=333.33 (round to 333)
    • Set d D d t = 0 \frac{dD}{dt} = 0 dtdD​=0: D ( − 0.25 + 0.0008 K ) = 0 D(-0.25 + 0.0008K) = 0 D(−0.25+0.0008K)=0
      • Either D = 0 D = 0 D=0 or K = 312.5 K = 312.5 K=312.5 (round to 313)
    • Equilibrium: ( K ∗ , D ∗ ) = ( 313 , 333 ) (K^*, D^*) = (313, 333) (K∗,D∗)=(313,333)

  3. Stability consideration:

    • Are populations close to equilibrium stable?
    • Or do they cycle (oscillate)?
    • In this model, small perturbations cause oscillations

Part B: Nullclines and Phase Portrait (20 minutes)

  1. Nullclines:

    • Kangaroo nullcline: K = 0 K = 0 K=0 or D = 333 D = 333 D=333
    • Dingo nullcline: D = 0 D = 0 D=0 or K = 313 K = 313 K=313

  2. Create a phase plane table:

    Region K K K vs. 313 D D D vs. 333 d K d t \frac{dK}{dt} dtdK​ d D d t \frac{dD}{dt} dtdD​ Trajectory
    I K < 313 K < 313 K<313 D < 333 D < 333 D<333 + − K increases, D decreases
    II K > 313 K > 313 K>313 D < 333 D < 333 D<333 + + Both increase
    III K > 313 K > 313 K>313 D > 333 D > 333 D>333 − + K decreases, D increases
    IV K < 313 K < 313 K<313 D > 333 D > 333 D>333 − − Both decrease

  3. Sketch the cyclic behavior:

    • Trajectories spiral around the equilibrium
    • High kangaroos → dingoes increase → kangaroos decline → dingoes decline → cycle repeats

Part C: Management Scenarios (15 minutes)

  1. Dingo culling: Pastoralists remove 40 dingoes/year to protect livestock

    • New equation: d D d t = − 0.25 D + 0.0008 K D − 40 \frac{dD}{dt} = -0.25D + 0.0008KD - 40 dtdD​=−0.25D+0.0008KD−40
    • Find new equilibrium: − 0.25 D + 0.0008 K D − 40 = 0 -0.25D + 0.0008KD - 40 = 0 −0.25D+0.0008KD−40=0
    • If D = 333 D = 333 D=333: − 0.25 ( 333 ) + 0.0008 K ( 333 ) − 40 = 0 -0.25(333) + 0.0008K(333) - 40 = 0 −0.25(333)+0.0008K(333)−40=0
    • Solve for new K K K equilibrium

  2. Effect of culling:

    • How much does kangaroo equilibrium increase?
    • What are the consequences for rangeland health?

  3. Alternative: Dingo protection

    • No culling: equilibrium remains at ( 313 , 333 ) (313, 333) (313,333)
    • Lower kangaroo numbers reduce grazing pressure
    • Write a brief (3-4 sentence) recommendation on dingo management for sustainable rangeland use

DELIVERABLES

  • Completed worksheet with equilibria and nullclines
  • Phase portrait showing cyclic trajectories
  • Group 7 (second presentation) in Week 9

PYTHON TEMPLATE

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.integrate import odeint

# Define the system
def predator_prey(state, t):
    K, D = state
    dK_dt = 0.5 * K - 0.0015 * K * D
    dD_dt = -0.25 * D + 0.0008 * K * D
    return [dK_dt, dD_dt]

# Equilibrium
K_star, D_star = 313, 333

# Phase plane
K_range = np.linspace(50, 600, 15)
D_range = np.linspace(50, 600, 15)
K_mesh, D_mesh = np.meshgrid(K_range, D_range)

dK_dt = 0.5 * K_mesh - 0.0015 * K_mesh * D_mesh
dD_dt = -0.25 * D_mesh + 0.0008 * K_mesh * D_mesh

# Normalize
magnitude = np.sqrt(dK_dt**2 + dD_dt**2)
dK_norm = dK_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)
dD_norm = dD_dt / (magnitude + 1e-10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

# Nullclines
plt.axhline(y=333, color='blue', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='Kangaroo nullcline (D=333)')
plt.axvline(x=313, color='red', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='Dingo nullcline (K=313)')
plt.plot(K_star, D_star, 'go', markersize=15, label=f'Equilibrium ({K_star}, {D_star})')

# Vector field
plt.quiver(K_mesh, D_mesh, dK_norm, dD_norm, alpha=0.5)

# Add a trajectory
t = np.linspace(0, 50, 1000)
initial_state = [350, 250]
trajectory = odeint(predator_prey, initial_state, t)
plt.plot(trajectory[:, 0], trajectory[:, 1], 'purple', linewidth=1.5, label='Sample trajectory')

plt.xlabel('Kangaroo Population (K)')
plt.ylabel('Dingo Population (D)')
plt.title('Predator-Prey Dynamics: Kangaroos and Dingoes')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 650)
plt.ylim(0, 650)
plt.show()
9

Week 9: Probability & Bayes' Theorem

COVID testing · False positives · Conditional probability

Week 9: Probability and Bayes' Theorem

PROBLEM BRIEF: W9

"COVID-19 Testing: Understanding False Positives"

SCENARIO

Western Australia is managing a COVID-19 outbreak. Health authorities are using rapid antigen tests (RATs) for community screening. Understanding test accuracy is critical for policy decisions.

Test characteristics:

  • Sensitivity: 85% (if you have COVID, test is positive 85% of the time)
  • Specificity: 95% (if you don't have COVID, test is negative 95% of the time)

Population prevalence: 2% of the tested population actually has COVID.

A person tests positive. What's the probability they actually have COVID?

YOUR TASK

Part A: Understanding Conditional Probability (15 minutes)

  1. Define the events:

    • D D D = person has COVID
    • T + T^+ T+ = test is positive

  2. Write out the given probabilities:

    • P ( T + ∣ D ) P(T^+ | D) P(T+∣D) = sensitivity = ?
    • P ( T − ∣ D c ) P(T^- | D^c) P(T−∣Dc) = specificity = ?
    • P ( D ) P(D) P(D) = prevalence = ?

  3. Calculate the complementary probabilities:

    • P ( T − ∣ D ) P(T^- | D) P(T−∣D) = false negative rate = ?
    • P ( T + ∣ D c ) P(T^+ | D^c) P(T+∣Dc) = false positive rate = ?
    • P ( D c ) P(D^c) P(Dc) = probability of not having COVID = ?

Part B: Applying Bayes' Theorem (25 minutes)

  1. We want to find P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+): the probability of having COVID given a positive test.

    Bayes' Theorem:

    P ( D ∣ T + ) = P ( T + ∣ D ) ⋅ P ( D ) P ( T + ) P(D | T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ | D) \cdot P(D)}{P(T^+)} P(D∣T+)=P(T+)P(T+∣D)⋅P(D)​

  2. Calculate the total probability of testing positive:

    P ( T + ) = P ( T + ∣ D ) ⋅ P ( D ) + P ( T + ∣ D c ) ⋅ P ( D c ) P(T^+) = P(T^+ | D) \cdot P(D) + P(T^+ | D^c) \cdot P(D^c) P(T+)=P(T+∣D)⋅P(D)+P(T+∣Dc)⋅P(Dc)

    Substitute the values and calculate.

  3. Now calculate P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+) using Bayes' theorem.

  4. Interpret: If someone tests positive, what's the chance they actually have COVID? Is it higher or lower than you expected?

Terminology: The quantity P(D | T+) you just calculated is called the Positive Predictive Value (PPV) — the probability that a person who tests positive truly has the disease. Similarly, the Negative Predictive Value (NPV) is P(D̄ | T−), the probability that a negative result is a true negative.

Part C: Sensitivity Analysis (15 minutes)

  1. How does prevalence affect the positive predictive value (PPV)?

    • Recalculate P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+) if prevalence increases to 10%
    • Recalculate if prevalence is only 0.5%
    • What pattern do you observe?

  2. Create a table:

    Prevalence P ( D ) P(D) P(D) P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+)
    0.5% ?
    2% ?
    5% ?
    10% ?
    20% ?

Part D: Policy Implications (5 minutes)

  1. The government is deciding between two testing strategies:

    • Strategy A — Test everyone in the community. The overall prevalence is 2%, so this is the prior probability that a randomly chosen person is infected.
    • Strategy B — Test only people who present with symptoms. Because symptomatic individuals are far more likely to be truly infected, the prevalence within this group is estimated at 15%.

    Which strategy gives more reliable positive results? Why?

  2. Write a brief (3-4 sentence) recommendation about when rapid tests are most useful and when they should be confirmed with PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) tests.

DELIVERABLES

  • Completed Group Worksheet with Bayes' theorem calculations
  • Graph showing P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+) vs. prevalence

HINTS & GUIDED STEPS

Bayes' Theorem intuition:

  • Prior probability: P ( D ) P(D) P(D) = what we know before the test
  • Likelihood: P ( T + ∣ D ) P(T^+ | D) P(T+∣D) = how good the test is
  • Posterior probability: P ( D ∣ T + ) P(D | T^+) P(D∣T+) = what we know after the test

Tree diagram approach:

Population (1000 people)
├─ Have COVID: 20 (2%)
│  ├─ Test positive: 17 (85% of 20)
│  └─ Test negative: 3
└─ No COVID: 980 (98%)
   ├─ Test positive: 49 (5% of 980)
   └─ Test negative: 931

Total positive tests: 17 + 49 = 66
Actually have COVID among positives: 17
P(D|T+) = 17/66 ≈ 0.26 = 26%

Python template:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Sensitivity and specificity (fixed)
sensitivity = 0.85
specificity = 0.95
false_positive_rate = 1 - specificity

# Range of prevalence values
prevalence = np.linspace(0.001, 0.3, 100)

# Calculate P(D|T+) for each prevalence
PPV = (sensitivity * prevalence) / (sensitivity * prevalence + false_positive_rate * (1 - prevalence))

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(prevalence * 100, PPV * 100, linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel('Prevalence (%)')
plt.ylabel('Positive Predictive Value (%)')
plt.title('How Prevalence Affects Test Reliability')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.axhline(y=50, color='r', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='50% threshold')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
10

Week 10: Mock Exam Workshop

Guided workshop · Hypothesis testing · Optimisation · Integration

Week 10: Mock Exam Workshop

ACTIVITY: W10

FORMAT

This week replaces the standard Problem Brief with a Guided Workshop. The instructor leads using slides — Q1 is fully worked as a class example, then groups tackle Q2 and Q3 before an interactive debrief.

Time Activity
0:00–0:20 Instructor walkthrough: hypothesis testing framework + Q1 fully worked as example on slides
0:20–1:00 Group work on Q2 (optimisation) and Q3 (integration — antiderivative hint provided)
1:00–2:00 Interactive debrief: instructor reveals answers step by step, asking each group for sub-steps

QUESTIONS

Work through these four questions in your group.

Question 1: Hypothesis Testing (12 marks)  — Instructor walkthrough on slides. Follow along and take notes.

A pharmaceutical company claims that their antibiotic is effective in at least 80% of cases. A hospital audit of 20 randomly selected patients who received the antibiotic found it was effective in only 11 cases.

Test whether this audit provides sufficient evidence to doubt the company's claim.

💡 Setting up the hypotheses — think about this before part (a)

Why is H0 the claim that p ≥ 0.80, not p ≤ 0.80?
Ask yourself: whose claim are we putting on trial? The company is making the bold assertion: "our antibiotic works at least 80% of the time." In hypothesis testing, the null hypothesis (H0) always represents the claim we are trying to find evidence against — it gets the benefit of the doubt, like innocent until proven guilty. So H0 carries the company's claim (p ≥ 0.80), and the alternative H1 is what we would conclude if the data are convincing enough to doubt it (p < 0.80).

Why a one-tailed test, not two-tailed?
A two-tailed test would ask: "is the effectiveness significantly different from 80% — whether higher or lower?" But we have no reason to be alarmed if the drug turns out to be more effective than claimed — that is good news for patients. We are only concerned if it performs worse than promised. So all of our suspicion points in one direction: the left tail. We are asking, "is the observed number of successes suspiciously low?" — not "suspiciously extreme in either direction?"

a) State the null hypothesis H0 and alternative hypothesis H1. Identify whether this is a left-tailed, right-tailed, or two-tailed test. (2 marks)

b) Let X be the number of patients for whom the antibiotic is effective. State the distribution of X under H0. Then calculate P(X ≤ 11), given the following individual probabilities:

  • P(X = 11) ≈ 0.0074
  • P(X = 10) ≈ 0.0020
  • P(X = 9) ≈ 0.0005
  • P(X ≤ 8) ≈ 0.0001 (negligible)

(4 marks)

c) At significance level α = 0.05 \alpha = 0.05 α=0.05, state whether you reject H0. Compare your p-value from part (b) to α and state your conclusion clearly. (4 marks)

d) Write a one-sentence conclusion in plain language that a hospital administrator could understand. What does the result mean for the company's claim? (2 marks)

Question 2: Optimization in Context (12 marks)

A dairy farm produces milk at a cost of C ( x ) = 2 x 2 − 80 x + 1200 C(x) = 2x^2 - 80x + 1200 C(x)=2x2−80x+1200 dollars, where x x x is thousands of liters produced per week. Milk sells for $1.20 per liter.

a) Write the profit function P ( x ) P(x) P(x) (3 marks)

b) Find the production level that maximizes profit (5 marks)

c) What is the maximum weekly profit? (2 marks)

d) If fuel costs increase and the cost function becomes C ( x ) = 2 x 2 − 80 x + 1500 C(x) = 2x^2 - 80x + 1500 C(x)=2x2−80x+1500, how does optimal production change? (2 marks)

Question 3: Integration Application (12 marks)

A solar panel's power output (in watts) varies throughout the day according to:

P ( t ) = 500 sin ⁡ ( π t 12 ) P(t) = 500\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{12}\right) P(t)=500sin(12πt​)

where t t t is hours after sunrise (0 ≤ t ≤ 12).

a) What is the peak power output and when does it occur? (3 marks)

b) Calculate the total energy produced during the day (energy = integral of power):

E = ∫ 0 12 P ( t )   d t E = \int_0^{12} P(t)\,dt E=∫012​P(t)dt

Hint — antiderivative provided:

\[\int \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{12}\right) dt = -\frac{12}{\pi} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{12}\right) + K\]

Your task: apply limits \(t = 0\) and \(t = 12\), evaluate \(\cos(\pi)\) and \(\cos(0)\), then simplify to find \(E\) in watt-hours.

(5 marks)

c) Express your answer in kilowatt-hours (kWh) (1 kWh = 1000 watt-hours) (2 marks)

d) At $0.30 per kWh, what is the daily revenue from this panel? (1 mark)

Question 4 — Optional Extension: Differential Equations

This question is optional and is not part of the weekly upload. Attempt it if time allows in the lab or at home for extra practice. Guidance, partial working, and the target answer are provided below.

A water tank drains according to the differential equation:

d V d t = − 0.5 V \frac{dV}{dt} = -0.5\sqrt{V} dtdV​=−0.5V​

where V V V is volume (liters) and t t t is time (minutes). Initially, V ( 0 ) = 100 V(0) = 100 V(0)=100 liters.

a) Separate variables and integrate to find V ( t ) V(t) V(t)

🟢 How to start — separating variables

Move all V terms to the left and all t terms to the right:

\[\frac{dV}{\sqrt{V}} = -0.5\,dt \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad V^{-1/2}\,dV = -0.5\,dt\]

Now integrate both sides. Recall that \(\int V^{-1/2}\,dV = 2V^{1/2} + C\).

Apply the initial condition \(V(0) = 100\) to find the constant, then rearrange for \(V(t)\).

Target answer: \(V(t) = (10 - 0.25t)^{2}\)

b) How long until the tank is half empty?

🟢 Hint: Set \(V(t) = 50\) and solve for \(t\). Take the positive square root before the tank empties.

Target answer: \(t \approx 11.7\) minutes

c) How long until the tank is completely empty?

🟢 Hint: Set \(V(t) = 0\).

Target answer: \(t = 40\) minutes

d) Sketch \(V(t)\) vs. \(t\) for \(0 \le t \le 40\), labelling the starting volume, the half-empty point, and the time at which the tank empties.

DELIVERABLES

Complete the group worksheet (Q1 notes + Q2 and Q3 full working) and submit it via the LMS by Tuesday 11:59 pm. Each group member submits their own copy.

Q4 is optional and is not submitted.

11

Week 11: Trigonometry

Pre-made scenario cards · Digital circular exchange · Sinusoidal modelling

Week 11: Trigonometry — Scenario-Based Peer Exchange

ACTIVITY: W11

Each group is assigned one of seven pre-made real-world scenario cards. All scenarios require the same mathematical toolkit — identifying parameters A, B, C, and D from data, writing a sinusoidal model, and answering applied questions — but set in different real-world contexts. In the second half of the lab, groups exchange work digitally and peer-review each other's solutions.

FORMAT

Time Activity
0:00 – 0:15 Demonstrator introduction — Standard sinusoidal form, parameter identification (A, B, C, D), degrees vs. radians
0:15 – 0:45 Part A (30 min) — Each group solves their assigned pre-made scenario card: build a sinusoidal model and answer all required questions
0:45 – 1:30 Part B (45 min) — Digital Circular Exchange — Upload Part A work to LMS; receive the previous group's scenario card and their Part A solution; review, verify, and extend
1:30 – 2:00 Debrief (30 min) — Demonstrator walks through a complete worked example; groups self-assess their models against the solution

STANDARD SINUSOIDAL FORM

All scenarios are modelled with the same standard form. You may use either sin or cos — both are acceptable, but sin is expected unless otherwise noted:

f ( t ) = A sin ⁡ ( B ( t − C ) ) + D f(t) = A\sin(B(t - C)) + D f(t)=Asin(B(t−C))+D

  • A — amplitude = (max − min) / 2
  • D — midline (vertical shift) = (max + min) / 2
  • B — angular frequency = 2π / P, where P is the period
  • C — phase shift: the value of t at which the sine function passes through the midline going upward (or the t-value at the peak if using cosine)

SCENARIO CARDS

Your demonstrator will assign one of the following cards to your group at the start of the lab. Each card is self-contained; all the data needed to determine the four parameters is provided.

Card 1 — Fremantle Tidal Heights

Tidal height at Fremantle Port varies approximately sinusoidally. On a particular day, low tide (0.2 m) occurs at 2:00 am and high tide (1.4 m) occurs at 8:00 am. The tidal pattern repeats every 12 hours. Let t = 0 correspond to midnight.

  1. Write h(t) = A sin(B(t − C)) + D for tidal height (m), where t is hours after midnight. State the values of A, B, C, and D, showing all working.
  2. Find the tidal height at 10:30 am. Show your working.
  3. Between 8:00 am and 8:00 pm, at what times is the tidal height below 0.5 m?

Card 2 — Perth Seasonal Temperature

The average daily maximum temperature in Perth follows a seasonal sinusoidal pattern. The maximum temperature of 33 °C occurs around January 15 (day 15 of the year) and the minimum of 17 °C occurs around July 16 (day 197). The period is 365 days.

  1. Write T(t) for temperature (°C), where t is the day of the year. Identify A, B, C, and D, showing all working. (Hint: cosine may be more natural for this card.)
  2. Estimate the average maximum temperature on April 1 (day 91).
  3. Find all days in the year when T(t) = 20 °C.

Card 3 — Perth Daylight Hours

The number of daylight hours in Perth varies sinusoidally. The longest day (13.9 hours of daylight) occurs around December 21 (day 355) and the shortest day (10.3 hours) occurs around June 21 (day 172). The period is 365 days.

  1. Write D(t) for daylight hours, where t is the day of the year. Identify A, B, C, and D, showing all working.
  2. How many hours of daylight does Perth receive on ANZAC Day (April 25, day 115)?
  3. On what days of the year does Perth experience more than 13.0 hours of daylight?

Card 4 — Exercise Heart Rate

During an interval cycling session, a student's heart rate oscillates between 95 bpm (low) and 165 bpm (peak). Peaks occur every 5 minutes, with the first peak at t = 1.25 minutes.

  1. Write h(t) = A sin(B(t − C)) + D for heart rate (bpm), where t is minutes. State A, B, C, and D with full working.
  2. What is the heart rate at t = 3.75 minutes?
  3. For how long (in seconds) per cycle is the heart rate above 150 bpm?

Card 5 — CO2 in a Greenhouse

In a sealed plant greenhouse, CO2 concentration varies sinusoidally due to photosynthesis (day) and respiration (night). Concentration peaks at 520 ppm at midnight and drops to a minimum of 380 ppm at noon. The period is 24 hours. Let t = 0 at midnight.

  1. Write c(t) for CO2 concentration (ppm), where t is hours after midnight. Identify A, B, C, and D with full working.
  2. What is the CO2 level at 3:00 pm?
  3. The photosynthesis rate is maximised when CO2 drops to 410 ppm. At what times during the day does this occur?

Card 6 — Ocean Wave Height

A wave buoy off Rottnest Island records wave height above mean sea level. The buoy records a maximum of 2.4 m and a minimum of 0.4 m. The period is 10 seconds, and a wave crest first occurs at t = 2.5 s.

  1. Write h(t) = A sin(B(t − C)) + D for wave height (m). State A, B, C, and D with full working.
  2. What is the wave height at t = 8 s?
  3. For what fraction of each 10-second period is the wave height above 2.0 m? Find the exact times within one period when h = 2.0 m.

Card 7 — Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO2) During Sleep

During healthy sleep, a patient's blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) follows a periodic pattern due to the breathing cycle. Readings peak at 98.5% and dip to 97.5%, with a period of 4 seconds. The first maximum occurs at t = 1 s.

  1. Write S(t) = A sin(B(t − C)) + D for SpO2 (%). State A, B, C, and D with full working.
  2. What is the SpO2 reading at t = 1.5 s?
  3. A monitor triggers an alarm when SpO2 drops below 98.1%. Over a 10-second observation window (t = 0 to t = 10), for how many seconds is the alarm triggered?

PART A — SOLVING YOUR SCENARIO (0:15 – 0:45)

Your demonstrator will announce which scenario card your group has been assigned at the start of the lab.

  1. Read your card carefully. Identify what each quantity represents (variable names, units, what the axes of a graph would be).
  2. Determine A, D, B, and C from the data provided, showing all working step-by-step.
  3. Write the full sinusoidal model equation, defining all symbols and stating units.
  4. Answer questions (b) and (c) with complete working, including equations and intermediate steps.
  5. Verify your model: substitute the given maximum and minimum values back into your equation to confirm they are reproduced.

Record all working clearly — your Part A sheet will be shared digitally with another group for peer review.

PART B — DIGITAL CIRCULAR EXCHANGE (0:45 – 1:30)

Exchange logistics:

  • At 0:45, each group emails their scenario card (raw data only — not their Part A working) to the group listed in the routing table below. The demonstrator facilitates this circular exchange.
  • You will receive the previous group's scenario card by email. The routing is circular:
Your group You receive work from
Group 1 Group 7 (or the last group in the lab)
Group 2 Group 1
Group 3 Group 2
Group 4 Group 3
Group 5 Group 4
Group 6 Group 5
Group 7 Group 6

⚠ Independent working required: Complete step 2 (your own full derivation) before opening the reviewed group’s Part A solution. Your demonstrator will be circulating to check this. Working seen in advance invalidates the peer-review task.

What to do in Part B:

  1. Read the scenario. Make sure you understand the context and what each variable represents. You are reviewing a different scenario from your own.
  2. Independently re-derive the model. Calculate A, B, C, and D yourself before looking at the group's working.
  3. Identify errors or confirm correctness. For each parameter, either confirm it is correct (with justification) or state the error and provide corrected working.
  4. Rewrite the model using the other trigonometric function. This is the extension task. If the reviewed group wrote their model using sine, express it equivalently using cosine. If they used cosine, convert to sine. Use the identity sin θ = cos(θ − π/2) or cos θ = sin(θ + π/2). The amplitude, period, and vertical shift stay the same — only the phase shift C changes by ± π/(2B). Show all working and state the complete new equation. Use the reviewed group’s values (corrected if necessary).

Be constructive and precise. A good peer review identifies specific issues, explains why something is correct or incorrect, and provides corrected working where needed.

ASSESSMENT

Week 11 counts as a double worksheet (25 marks total, weighted as 2× a normal lab).

Part Weight Marks What is assessed
Part A — Your scenario solution 50% 12.5 Correct parameter identification with working; accurate model equation; correct answers to (b) and (c); model verification
Part B — Peer review quality 50% 12.5 Accurate independent verification of each parameter; clear identification of any errors (or confirmation of correctness with justification); extension question answered with full working

Marking focus:

  • Mathematical correctness and completeness of working — final answers alone are not sufficient
  • Precision of language: equations should define symbols, state units, and label variables
  • For Part B: quality of feedback — explain why something is correct or incorrect, not just “correct” or “wrong”

DELIVERABLES

  • Part A Worksheet — Uploaded to LMS by Tuesday 11:59 pm (Week 11 → Part A Submission)
  • Part B Peer Review Sheet — Submitted to LMS by Tuesday 11:59 pm (Week 11 → Part B Submission)
  • Part A includes: all parameter derivations with working, full model equation with units, answers to (b) and (c), and model verification
  • Part B includes: independent parameter verification, identification of any errors (with corrections), and extension question answered with full working
12

Week 12: Linear Programming

4 LP problems · Graphical method · Corner-point theorem · Group Workthrough

Week 12: Linear Programming — Group Workthrough

This week each pair of groups solves a different real-world linear programming problem using the graphical method. At the end of the session the demonstrator leads a whole-class workthrough comparing all four solutions.

FORMAT

Time Activity
0:00 – 0:15 LP Review — Demonstrator recaps LP formulation and the graphical solution method
0:15 – 1:00 Group Work (45 min) — Each group solves their assigned LP problem (Parts A, B, and C)
1:00 – 1:40 Group Workthrough (40 min) — Demonstrator leads whole-class walkthrough; all four solutions compared
1:40 – 2:00 Debrief (20 min) — Discussion: how do different problem contexts affect the feasible region and optimal solution?

KEY LP CONCEPTS

Every linear program has three components:

  1. Decision variables — the quantities you control (x, y)
  2. Objective function — what you maximise or minimise: Z = c₁x + c₂y
  3. Constraints — linear inequalities bounding the decisions, plus non-negativity: x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0
Corner-Point Theorem: If an optimal solution exists, it occurs at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region. Evaluate Z at every corner point — pick the best one.

8-step graphical solution method:

  1. Define decision variables.
  2. Write the objective function (max or min).
  3. Write each constraint as a linear inequality.
  4. Add non-negativity: x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
  5. Graph each boundary line; shade the feasible region.
  6. Find all corner points (solve pairs of boundary equations simultaneously).
  7. Build a corner-point table: evaluate Z at each corner.
  8. Report the optimal solution and interpret in context.

PROBLEM ASSIGNMENTS

Problem Groups Title Type
W12A 1 – 2 Sustainable Campus Dining: Guild Menu Minimise cost
W12B 3 – 4 Crop Allocation: Wheat vs Chickpeas Maximise gross margin
W12C 5 – 6 Hospital Staffing: Nurses vs Technicians Minimise wage index
W12D 7 – 8 Biodiversity Corridor: Wetland vs Forest Maximise biodiversity score

LP PROBLEM BRIEFS

Your demonstrator will assign one of the following problems to your group at the start of the session. Each card is self-contained; all data needed to formulate and solve the LP is provided.

W12A — Sustainable Campus Dining: Guild Menu Planning

Groups 1–2  ·  Minimise cost

The UWA Student Guild is redesigning its food court menu to meet sustainability and nutrition goals. Two meal types are on offer:

  • x = plant-based serves per week (cost: $2.50/serve)
  • y = chicken serves per week (cost: $4.00/serve)

Problem brief (verbal data): The Guild wants the lowest possible weekly food cost while still meeting nutrition and sustainability targets. Each plant-based serve contributes 18 g protein, 220 kcal, 3 mg iron, and 0.5 kg CO₂. Each chicken serve contributes 30 g protein, 280 kcal, 1 mg iron, and 2.5 kg CO₂. Across the week, the menu must provide at least 350 g protein, at least 3500 kcal, and at least 25 mg iron, while keeping total food-related emissions to no more than 20 kg CO₂. Serves cannot be negative.

Worksheet tasks:

  1. Formulate the LP: define decision variables; write the objective function and all constraint inequalities.
  2. Graph all constraint boundaries on the same axes; shade the feasible region; identify and calculate all corner points.
  3. Evaluate Z at each corner point; state the optimal solution and minimum cost. Sensitivity: how does the solution change if the carbon limit tightens to ≤ 15 kg CO₂?

W12B — Crop Allocation: Wheat vs Chickpeas

Groups 3–4  ·  Maximise gross margin

A farm has 100 ha to allocate between wheat and chickpeas, subject to land, water, labour, and policy constraints:

  • x = hectares of wheat (gross margin: $400/ha)
  • y = hectares of chickpeas (gross margin: $300/ha)

Problem brief (verbal data): The farmer wants to maximise weekly gross margin. Wheat returns $400 per hectare; chickpeas return $300 per hectare. The farm has at most 100 ha available in total. Water is limited to 240 ML, with wheat requiring 3 ML/ha and chickpeas requiring 2 ML/ha. Labour is limited to 340 hours, with wheat requiring 2 h/ha and chickpeas requiring 4 h/ha. To satisfy a crop-diversity policy, chickpea area cannot exceed 1.5 times wheat area. An export contract also requires at least 20 ha of wheat. Areas cannot be negative.

Worksheet tasks:

  1. Formulate the LP: define decision variables; write the objective function and all constraint inequalities.
  2. Graph all constraint boundaries on the same axes; shade the feasible region; identify and calculate all corner points.
  3. Evaluate Z at each corner point; state the optimal allocation and maximum gross margin. Sensitivity: how does the solution change if water availability is reduced to 210 ML?

W12C — Hospital Staffing: Minimising Staffing Wage Index

Groups 5–6  ·  Minimise wage index

A hospital ward must schedule nurse and technician shifts each week to meet coverage, patient care, and technical task requirements at minimum staffing cost:

  • x = nurse shifts per week (cost or wage index: 8 per shift)
  • y = technician shifts per week (cost or wage index: 6 per shift)

Problem brief (verbal data): Clinical Staffing Brief: The Ward 12CA post-surgical ward manager must optimise the weekly roster to minimise the Staffing Wage Index (Z).

  • Team and wage index: Registered Nurses (x) have a wage index of 8 per shift, and Clinical Technicians (y) have a wage index of 6 per shift.
  • Total coverage: At least 20 total shifts are required each week to maintain baseline ward safety.
  • Patient acuity: High-care patients require at least 36 care units. Each nurse shift contributes 2 care units, and each technician shift contributes 1 care unit.
  • Diagnostics: Technical workload requires at least 24 task units. Each technician shift contributes 3 task units, and each nurse shift contributes 1 task unit.
  • Safety policy: Nurse shifts must be at least 50% of technician shifts (supervision ratio).
  • Resource cap: Nurse shifts are capped at 18 due to recruitment limits.
  • Feasibility: Shift counts cannot be negative.

Worksheet tasks:

  1. Formulate the LP: define decision variables; write the objective function and all constraint inequalities.
  2. Graph all constraint boundaries on the same axes; shade the feasible region; identify and calculate all corner points.
  3. Evaluate Z at each corner point; state the optimal staffing mix and minimum wage index. Sensitivity: how does the solution change if the acuity requirement increases to 40 units?

W12D — Biodiversity Corridor: Wetland vs Forest Restoration

Groups 7–8  ·  Maximise biodiversity score

A conservation agency has 80 ha of degraded corridor to restore, subject to ecological and regulatory constraints:

  • x = ha of wetland restored (biodiversity score: 5 per ha)
  • y = ha of upland forest restored (biodiversity score: 3 per ha)

Problem brief (verbal data): The agency wants to maximise biodiversity score. Wetland restoration yields 5 biodiversity points per hectare, and upland forest restoration yields 3 points per hectare. Total restored area cannot exceed 80 ha. The restoration budget is capped at $240k, with wetland costing $4k/ha and upland forest costing $2k/ha. To protect catchment hydrology, annual water impact must not exceed 180 ML, with wetland contributing 1 ML/ha and upland forest contributing 3 ML/ha. For ecosystem balance, upland forest area must be at least half the wetland area. Bird habitat protection requires at least 18 ha of wetland restoration. Areas cannot be negative.

Worksheet tasks:

  1. Formulate the LP: define decision variables; write the objective function and all constraint inequalities.
  2. Graph all constraint boundaries on the same axes; shade the feasible region; identify and calculate all corner points.
  3. Evaluate Z at each corner point; state the optimal restoration plan and maximum biodiversity score. Sensitivity: how does the solution change if the budget is reduced to $160k (4x + 2y ≤ 160)?

JUPYTER NOTEBOOK (INDEPENDENT STUDY)

Notebook: Week12_SES_LP_Complete.ipynb — formative, not graded. Complete before or after the lab session to build LP skills.

Part Title What you do
A Market Equilibrium & Surplus Solve simultaneous supply/demand equations; compute consumer and producer surplus
B Diet LP Problem Formulate a two-variable LP; graph constraints; find corner points
C Land Allocation Multi-constraint LP with sensitivity analysis

Part B is the core skill for the worksheet — make sure you can graph constraint boundaries, shade the feasible region, and evaluate Z at each corner point before the lab.

SUBMISSION & WORKTHROUGH

Details
Submit Group Worksheet + graph + corner-point table
Due: Tuesday 2 June, 11:59 pm (post-SWOTVAC non-standard deadline)
Workthrough No formal presentations this week. The demonstrator leads a whole-class walkthrough comparing corner points and solutions across all four problems (W12A–D).
📋

Resources

Templates and guidance for all lab submissions

5. Group Worksheet Template

Your group should complete one worksheet per week and submit it via LMS.

Grading: Worksheets receive a group mark. Your individual mark is adjusted by your peer-reviewed contribution score (see Section 2.4).

SCIE1500 GROUP WORKSHEET – WEEK ___

Group Number: ________

  • Python code
  • Additional graphs
  • AI chat transcripts
  • Extended calculations

6. Presentation Rubric

Your group presentation will be graded out of 20 marks (scaled to 4% of your unit grade).

Important: This rubric determines your group mark. Your individual mark will be calculated by multiplying the group mark by your peer-reviewed contribution score (see Section 2.4).

Example: If your group receives 16/20 (80%) and your contribution score is 90%, your individual mark is 80 × 0.90 = 72%.

Criterion Excellent (5) Good (3-4) Developing (1-2) Poor (0)
Problem Context (5 marks) Clear explanation of real-world problem; audience understands why it matters Problem explained but lacks context or motivation Problem stated but unclear why it's important No clear problem statement
Mathematical Approach (5 marks) Correct model/method selected; clear justification for choice; formula presented clearly Correct approach with minor errors; justification present but weak Approach has errors or is poorly justified Wrong method or no mathematical content
Results & Visualization (5 marks) Results clearly presented with effective graphs/equations; calculations correct Results mostly correct; visualization adequate Results unclear or visualization ineffective Major errors or no results shown
Interpretation & Implications (3 marks) Clear connection to real-world decision; discusses limitations and trade-offs Some interpretation but lacks depth Minimal interpretation; just states numbers No interpretation of results
Delivery & Timing (2 marks) All members participate; well-practiced; within 10-minute limit Most members participate; slightly over/under time Uneven participation; timing issues One person dominates; major timing problems

Total: ____ / 20 marks

7. Tips for Success

7.1 During Lab Sessions

Do:

  • Arrive on time with materials ready
  • Contribute actively to your group
  • Ask questions when you're stuck
  • Try the mathematical formulation before coding
  • Verify your answers make sense in context

Don't:

  • Let one person do all the work
  • Copy from other groups without understanding
  • Rely entirely on AI tools
  • Skip the interpretation step
  • Leave before the wrap-up

7.2 For Presentations

Do:

  • Divide roles fairly (everyone speaks)
  • Practice timing beforehand
  • Focus on the "so what?"
  • Use visuals effectively
  • Anticipate questions

Don't:

  • Show raw Python code
  • Read slides word-for-word
  • Go over the 10-minute limit
  • Assume the audience remembers details
  • Present without rehearsing

7.3 Problem-Solving Strategy

  1. Read carefully – What is the question really asking?
  2. Identify knowns and unknowns – What information do you have?
  3. Choose a method – What technique from lectures applies?
  4. Set up mathematically – Write equations before coding
  5. Solve/compute – Do the math (by hand or Python)
  6. Check reasonableness – Does your answer make sense?
  7. Interpret – What does this mean in the real world?

7.4 Common Pitfalls

Pitfall How to Avoid
Starting with code before understanding the problem Always formulate mathematically first
Not checking units Track units through every calculation
Ignoring domain/range restrictions Ask: "What values make sense here?"
Over-relying on AI Use AI to check/debug, not to think for you
Skipping the "so what?" Always connect math back to the scenario

7.5 Using AI Responsibly

Good AI use:

  • "Why is my Python giving a syntax error on line 12?"
  • "How do I plot two functions on the same axes in matplotlib?"
  • "Check my derivative: is d d x [ x 2 e x ] = 2 x e x + x 2 e x \frac{d}{dx}[x^2 e^x] = 2xe^x + x^2e^x dxd​[x2ex]=2xex+x2ex?"

Bad AI use:

  • [Pasting entire Problem Brief] "Solve this for me"
  • Using AI-generated code you don't understand
  • Not documenting AI usage in your worksheet

7.6 Study Habits

Weekly:

  • Review lecture notes before lab
  • Complete the Problem Brief during lab time
  • Review the presentation if you're in the audience
  • Attempt practice problems from the textbook

Before Exams:

  • Rework all Problem Briefs from scratch
  • Practice Part II exam questions under timed conditions
  • Focus on interpretation, not just calculation
  • Review presentation feedback for common errors

7.7 Getting Help

During labs:

  • Ask your demonstrator
  • Discuss with your group
  • Use the AI tutor in the course app

Outside labs:

  • Unit Coordinator office hours
  • STUDYSmarter drop-in sessions
  • Discussion board on LMS
  • Peer study groups

Quick Reference: Semester Schedule

Week Topic Lab Activity Presenting Groups
0 Mathematical Foundations Python basics, Schaefer model –
1 Functions Plastic production analysis –
2 Exponential & Logarithmic Growth and decay models –
3 Logistic & Schaefer Fishery management 1, 2
4 Limits & Derivatives Chemical optimization 3, 4
5 Optimization Wildlife corridor design 5, 6
6 Integration Carbon sequestration 7, 1
7 Definite Integrals & Surplus Market welfare analysis 2, 3
8 Lotka-Volterra Predator-prey dynamics 4, 5
9 Probability & Bayes COVID testing 6, 7
10 Hypothesis Testing Mock Exam Workshop All (no presentations)
11 Trigonometry Peer Review Exchange All (no presentations)
12 Linear Programming Group Workthrough All (no presentations)

Good luck with your labs! Remember: the goal isn't just to get the right answer—it's to learn how analytical scientists think, formulate, and communicate.
